title: Home Equity Line of Credit (HELOC) in Greater Vancouver
metaTitle: HELOC in Greater Vancouver — Up to 65% LTV Standalone
metaDescription: Home Equity Line of Credit in Greater Vancouver. Up to 65% LTV standalone, 80% combined with mortgage. Variable rate, interest-only payments, revolving access.
intro: A HELOC is a revolving credit line secured against your home's equity. Lower rate than unsecured credit, flexible access, interest-only payments — when used well, it's the most flexible financial tool a homeowner has.

body.heading: How a HELOC works
paragraphs: Your HELOC is registered against your property like a mortgage, but it works like a credit card: you can borrow up to a credit limit, repay any time without penalty, and re-borrow as needed. The minimum payment is typically interest-only, calculated at prime + a margin (usually prime + 0.5% to prime + 1%).

Standalone HELOCs (where you have no first mortgage) cap at 65% loan-to-value. Combined HELOC + mortgage products cap at 80% LTV — up to 65% can be the HELOC portion. As you pay down the mortgage, room expands automatically into the HELOC.

body.heading: Smart and not-so-smart uses
paragraphs: Smart: emergency reserve, renovation funding, opportunistic investment property purchases, smoothing self-employed income, bridge financing, debt consolidation when refinancing isn't worth the penalty. Not so smart: vacations, lifestyle creep, anything that won't appreciate or generate income to repay it.
bullets: Variable rate at prime + 0.5% to prime + 1.0% (much cheaper than unsecured credit)
Interest-only minimum payment (you can always pay more)
Re-borrow up to your limit any time — like a credit card
Tax-deductible interest if used for investment purposes

faqs.question: What's the difference between a HELOC and a mortgage?
faqs.answer: A mortgage is a fixed-amount loan with scheduled principal-and-interest payments. A HELOC is a revolving credit line — borrow, repay, re-borrow at will, with interest-only minimum payments. Most clients benefit from having both: a mortgage for the bulk of the financing, a HELOC for flexibility.

faqs.question: Will a HELOC affect my credit score?
faqs.answer: Yes — a HELOC application includes a hard credit pull. Once open, utilization affects your score (high utilization hurts; low utilization helps). Most lenders report HELOC balances and limits to credit bureaus.

faqs.question: Can I get a HELOC if I'm self-employed?
faqs.answer: Yes — but stated-income or BFS HELOC programs are narrower than mortgage programs. Prepare for a 2-year financial history at minimum. Some lenders offer equity-based HELOCs that look more at the property than the income.

keywords: home equity line of credit BC
